ai_strategy_lead.name: Robert Cummings
ai_strategy_lead.title: Executive Director of Academic Innovation; Chair, AI Task Force

documents.title: AI Task Force (eGrove archive of task-force meeting recordings and artifacts)
University of Mississippi Libraries Artificial Intelligence Guide (Faculty Resources)

ai_strategy.summary: UM has no single published campus-wide AI strategy. Strategy is being advanced through the faculty-led AI Task Force (chaired by Robert Cummings, Executive Director of Academic Innovation), which is drafting suggested best-practice guidelines for the Provost across teaching, research, service, and business practices. Ole Miss deliberately declined a one-size-fits-all policy in favor of department-level AI policies expected to mature into fall 2026, and has amplified its posture through two flagship partnerships: OpenAI's NextGenAI consortium (March 2025) and the Mid-South AI Research Consortium (April 2026).

key_provisions: Departments (not individual professors) responsible for AI-use policies in coursework
CETL Syllabus Template (p. 9) provides recommended AI statements for syllabi
'Stoplight' (green/yellow/red) template for categorizing acceptable AI tools in courses
DEER framework (Define, Evaluate, Encourage, Reflect) guides AI-inclusive assignment design
University Libraries do not recommend use of AI detection tools due to false-positive risk
Existing IT computing-use policy governs cloud services generally; no AI-specific AUP yet
ai_policy.summary: UM's institutional posture toward AI is permissive and distributed rather than prescriptive. The AI Task Force's teaching-and-learning subcommittee, chaired by CETL senior director Joshua Eyler, has pushed AI policy-making down to the departmental level across the College of Liberal Arts and other units, rather than issuing a single standardized acceptable-use policy. The Libraries and CETL jointly provide faculty-facing frameworks (DEER, stoplight syllabus template) and explicitly counsel against AI detection tools.

bodies.name: University of Mississippi AI Task Force
bodies.scope: Campus-wide; four subcommittees covering teaching and learning, research, service, and business practices. Chaired by Robert Cummings (Executive Director of Academic Innovation). Members include faculty, data scientists, and representatives from the Office of Research and Sponsored Programs. Meets monthly/quarterly; drafting suggested guidelines for the Provost.

bodies.name: AI Task Force – Teaching and Learning Subcommittee
bodies.scope: Chaired by Joshua Eyler (Senior Director, Center for Excellence in Teaching and Learning). Recommended department-level AI policy approach and coordinates faculty development.

bodies.name: UM Libraries Artificial Intelligence Committee
bodies.scope: Library faculty committee developing AI research/learning guides, recommending DEER and stoplight frameworks, and fielding AI instruction questions.

bodies.name: AI Policy Board (internal, in formation)
bodies.scope: Referenced by Robert Cummings as the venue for policy conversations emerging from the AI Task Force. Public details limited.

governance_structure.summary: UM's AI governance is anchored in a faculty-led AI Task Force with four functional subcommittees, supplemented by a Libraries AI Committee and an internal AI Policy Board referenced in task-force updates. There is no chief AI officer or dedicated AI office; the Executive Director of Academic Innovation (in the Division of Academic Affairs) functions as the de facto AI strategy lead.

initiatives.title: Founding member of OpenAI's NextGenAI consortium
initiatives.description: On March 4, 2025, UM was named one of 15 founding institutions in OpenAI's $50M NextGenAI consortium. Ole Miss is Mississippi's only institution in the consortium and one of three SEC members (alongside Georgia and Texas A&M). An initial $50,000 in OpenAI research funding flows through a competitive grant process run by the National Center for Narrative Intelligence with the Center for Practical Ethics and the Institute for Data Science.
initiatives.significance: Places an R1 flagship in a small rural/Deep-South state directly into the OpenAI research partner network, signalling that elite AI-industry partnerships are no longer confined to coastal R1s.

investment.description: $50,000 initial tranche to UM as part of OpenAI's $50M total NextGenAI commitment across 15 institutions; additional API credits and research-fellowship access.

collaborations.description: Multi-institutional research and education consortium convened by OpenAI.

tools_and_vendors.name: ChatGPT / GPT APIs
tools_and_vendors.role: Research platform and API credits provided to consortium members

initiatives.policy_implications: Opens institutional research access to frontier LLMs, raising both opportunity and scrutiny around data governance and IRB review of AI-augmented research.

initiatives.title: Co-founder of the Mid-South AI Research Consortium
initiatives.description: On April 20, 2026, UM joined the University of Arkansas, University of Memphis, and University of Tennessee Health Science Center in Memphis to launch the Mid-South AI Research Consortium, aggregating more than 300 AI-adjacent researchers across four R1s. The consortium uses a low-overhead model designed for joint federal grant applications, shared digital environments, and coordinated workforce upskilling.
initiatives.significance: One of the first cross-state R1 AI consortia in the Southeast, framing the Mid-South as a 'Digital Delta' focused on rural and precision health, supply chain and logistics, energy and data centers, agriculture and food security, and national defense.

investment.funding_source: Institutional cost-sharing; planned joint federal grant applications
investment.description: No total investment disclosed; consortium is explicitly a 'low-overhead' coordination body rather than a new center with a named endowment.

partners: University of Tennessee Health Science Center
collaborations.description: Four R1 universities forming an applied-AI research and workforce alliance across MS, AR, and TN.

initiatives.policy_implications: Shared computing environments imply a forthcoming coordinated data-governance and IP-sharing framework across three state systems.

initiatives.title: National Center for Narrative Intelligence (NCNI)
initiatives.description: Established in 2023 as the first center of its kind in the US, NCNI pioneers the application of AI to analyze how online narratives and disinformation shape perception, with applications in journalism, healthcare, national security, and public policy. Co-directed by Andrea Hickerson (Dean, School of Journalism and New Media) and Wes Jennings (Department of Criminal Justice and Legal Studies). Primary tech partner: Mississippi-based EdgeTheory.
initiatives.significance: A rare example of an R1 building a dedicated applied-AI center around disinformation and narrative analytics rather than a generic 'AI institute,' and the vehicle through which OpenAI funds now flow.

investment.funding_source: Institutional + private-sector (EdgeTheory) + federal grants + NextGenAI pass-through
investment.description: Specific founding budget not disclosed; center is positioned as UM's flagship AI research unit.

collaborations.description: AI-powered narrative tracking and synthesis platform supplied by Mississippi-based EdgeTheory.

tools_and_vendors.name: EdgeTheory narrative-intelligence platform
tools_and_vendors.role: Live narrative tracking, synthesis, and disinformation analytics

initiatives.policy_implications: NCNI's work intersects with national-security and information-integrity policy debates; ethics oversight is partnered through UM's Center for Practical Ethics.

initiatives.title: NSF-funded baseline model for AI ethics education
initiatives.description: In 2024, UM's Center for Practical Ethics (director: Deborah Mower) received a ~$400,000 three-year NSF Ethics and Responsible Research Program award (2418866/67/68) to build the first baseline model for AI ethics education. Partners: Virginia Tech (Qin Zhu) and Texas A&M (Glen Miller). Delivery sites include 15 community colleges and universities via the Mississippi AI Network plus 10 Texas A&M system institutions.
initiatives.significance: First NSF Ethics and Responsible Research Program grant ever awarded to a Mississippi institution, and a national model that other universities can adopt wholesale rather than build from scratch.

investment.description: NSF grants 2418866/67/68 totaling approximately $400,000 over three years, shared among UM, Texas A&M, and Virginia Tech.

collaborations.description: Three-institution NSF team, with delivery through the statewide Mississippi AI Network of 15 community colleges/universities.

initiatives.policy_implications: Produces pedagogical templates and ethics frameworks intended for adoption across many institutions, including Mississippi's two-year sector.

initiatives.title: Mississippi AI Institute for Teachers
initiatives.description: Founded by the Department of Writing and Rhetoric with seed funding from the Institute for Data Science, with partners CETL and the Academic Innovations Group. Runs summer and winter cohorts (including a January 2024 AI Winter Institute) building applied and critical AI literacy for UM faculty and Mississippi K-12 teachers. Featured in the Washington Post.
initiatives.significance: One of the earliest faculty-AI-literacy training programs at a US R1, and an explicit bridge between UM faculty development and Mississippi K-12 teacher upskilling.

investment.funding_source: Institute for Data Science (seed) + departmental + CETL operating budget
investment.description: Seed funding amount not disclosed; operating on institutional budgets with open-access curriculum published online.

collaborations.description: Draws teachers from across Mississippi for multi-day AI literacy institutes.

tools_and_vendors.name: ChatGPT
tools_and_vendors.role: Primary generative-AI platform referenced in curriculum

initiatives.policy_implications: Feeds into a voluntary, department-led policy model rather than a central mandate.

initiatives.title: Department-level AI coursework policy framework
initiatives.description: Since late 2024, UM has pushed AI-use policy-making from individual professors to academic departments. The AI Task Force teaching-and-learning subcommittee, chaired by Joshua Eyler (CETL Senior Director), recommended the shift; the College of Liberal Arts' 21 programs and the School of Journalism and New Media are sequencing their own policies through fall 2026. Faculty are equipped with the DEER framework and a stoplight (green/yellow/red) template.
initiatives.significance: A unusual middle path in the sector: rather than a campus-wide AUP or purely syllabus-level discretion, UM formalizes the department as the unit of AI policy-making.

investment.description: No dedicated budget line; work is embedded in CETL and Academic Innovation operating budgets.

initiatives.policy_implications: Produces a patchwork of AI policies by department; may force downstream reconciliation for shared services (library, advising, honor code).

initiatives.title: Bachelor of Science in Data Science (School of Engineering)
initiatives.description: UM's School of Engineering offers a 126-credit-hour, four-year B.S. in Data Science with two emphasis options (Computer Science and Data Science) and experiential learning via co-ops, internships, hackathons, and ACM. Curriculum draws on CS, mathematics, and statistics and includes coursework in machine learning and high-performance computing.
initiatives.significance: Places a data-science/AI pipeline on the undergraduate side at an SEC flagship where the humanities have historically led campus AI conversations.

investment.description: Funded through the School of Engineering's regular instructional budget; external industry co-op partners.

collaborations.description: Structured co-ops and internships are a required program component.

initiatives.policy_implications: Expands the university's credentialed AI/data workforce pipeline in a state that has made AI workforce a top economic priority (see MAI-TAP).

initiatives.title: Advanced AI and AI coursework (catalog)
initiatives.description: UM maintains dedicated AI courses including ENGR 662 Advanced Artificial Intelligence (Spring 2024-25) and discipline 110102 Artificial Intelligence (Spring 2025-26), covering logical foundations of AI, machine learning, planning, knowledge representation, and image understanding.
initiatives.significance: Demonstrates sustained AI curriculum across engineering rather than one-off electives, which anchors UM's participation in state-level AI workforce plans.

investment.description: Embedded in School of Engineering and other academic-unit instructional budgets.

initiatives.title: Institute for Data Science as campus AI/ML hub
initiatives.description: The Institute for Data Science (IDS), led by director Panduka Nagahawatte, is UM's interdisciplinary data-science and machine-learning hub. It serves as a grant-writing engine, houses a dedicated data scientist (Lori Nichols), and coordinates affiliated faculty across counselor education, pharmacy administration, nutrition, computer science, health sciences, and psychology. IDS provided seed funding for the Mississippi AI Institute for Teachers and is one of three units through which NextGenAI money flows.
initiatives.significance: Functions as the connective tissue for UM's AI work: the place where domain departments, workforce programs, and external partners intersect.

investment.description: Regular operating budget plus sponsored-research flow-through; specific totals not public.

collaborations.description: Institute welcomes partnerships with external organizations for ethical data applications.

initiatives.policy_implications: Central touchpoint for research-data-governance conversations around AI.

initiatives.title: Mississippi Center for Supercomputing Research (MCSR) AI/GPU compute
initiatives.description: MCSR, housed at UM, operates Maple (Cray cluster; ~382 TFLOPS peak; 140 nodes; 5,454 cores; 29 NVIDIA Kepler K20, 4 Tesla P100, and 12 Tesla V100S GPUs; 27.1 TB memory) and Magnolia (HPE Apollo 2000; 50 nodes including 6 dual-GPU nodes; HDR100 InfiniBand). Resources are available to faculty, staff, and students across all Mississippi public IHLs.
initiatives.significance: Provides R1-grade GPU compute not just to UM but to the entire Mississippi public university system, making UM an unusually important compute provider for a mid-sized R1.

investment.funding_source: State and institutional funding; federal grants
investment.description: Totals not disclosed; runs as a multi-institutional shared resource.

collaborations.description: Mississippi public university system-wide shared HPC resource.

tools_and_vendors.name: Magnolia
tools_and_vendors.role: Secondary cluster with GPU nodes and HDR100 InfiniBand

initiatives.title: Role in Mississippi AI Talent Accelerator Program (MAI-TAP)
initiatives.description: Governor Tate Reeves announced the $9.1M MAI-TAP program in June 2025 to build AI/ML workforce capacity at Mississippi higher-education institutions in support of an AWS expansion. UM did not receive a direct MAI-TAP grant award, but is active in the supporting ecosystem through existing funding, the Mississippi AI Network, and the Mid-South AI Research Consortium.
initiatives.significance: Shows UM's role in a state-level AI workforce strategy anchored to a named hyperscaler (AWS) investment, even without a direct state award.

investment.funding_source: State of Mississippi (AccelerateMS + MDA) in partnership with AWS
investment.description: Statewide $9.1M program; UM not a direct grantee but participates in collaborative workforce pillars.

initiatives.policy_implications: Ties UM's AI workforce pipeline to AWS and to state industrial policy.

initiatives.title: Participation in the Mississippi AI Network (MAIN)
initiatives.description: UM is a university partner in MAIN, the nation's first statewide AI workforce initiative, spearheaded by Mississippi Gulf Coast Community College with Intel and Dell Technologies. MAIN offers free self-paced AI courses statewide, having reached tens of thousands of higher-ed employees, 1,100+ K-12 teachers, and workforce members in all sectors.
initiatives.significance: Embeds UM in a nationally-novel state-scale AI workforce architecture that crosses the K-12, community college, four-year, and industry boundaries.

investment.funding_source: State of Mississippi + industry partners (Intel, Dell, AWS)
investment.description: Funding flows through multiple state and industry channels; no UM-specific line item published.

partners: Mississippi public universities (MSU, USM, JSU, Alcorn State, Delta State, MUW)
collaborations.description: First statewide multi-sector AI alliance in the US.

initiatives.policy_implications: Creates a common AI curriculum layer across Mississippi public education.

research_centers.name: National Center for Narrative Intelligence (NCNI)
research_centers.focus: Applied AI for narrative, disinformation, and communication research across journalism, healthcare, national security, and public policy.

research_centers.name: Institute for Data Science (IDS)
research_centers.focus: Interdisciplinary data science and machine learning hub; grant writing, student training, industry partnerships.

research_centers.name: Center for Practical Ethics
research_centers.focus: Applied ethics, including AI ethics education (NSF ER2 grant) and rural-healthcare ethics.

research_centers.name: Mississippi Center for Supercomputing Research (MCSR)
research_centers.focus: Shared HPC/AI compute resource for UM and all Mississippi public IHLs.

Digital Media Studies minor (AI and data science emphasis in development)

enterprise_platforms.product: ChatGPT / GPT APIs (via NextGenAI research access)
Gemini (access under consideration by AI Task Force)

computing_infrastructure.summary: Through the Mississippi Center for Supercomputing Research (MCSR), UM operates Maple, a ~382 TFLOPS Cray cluster with 45 NVIDIA GPUs (29 Kepler K20, 4 Tesla P100, 12 Tesla V100S) across 140 compute nodes, and Magnolia, an HPE Apollo 2000 cluster with 50 nodes including 6 dual-GPU nodes linked over HDR100 InfiniBand. The resource is shared across all Mississippi public IHLs, making UM a system-level AI compute provider.

investments_and_gifts.title: OpenAI NextGenAI research funding
investments_and_gifts.purpose: Competitive research grant process run by NCNI, Center for Practical Ethics, and IDS.

investments_and_gifts.title: NSF Ethics and Responsible Research grants (2418866/67/68) for AI ethics education
investments_and_gifts.purpose: Build a baseline model of AI ethics education across higher education (UM lead, with Texas A&M and Virginia Tech).

investments_and_gifts.title: Mississippi AI Talent Accelerator Program (statewide)
investments_and_gifts.source: State of Mississippi (AccelerateMS, MDA) with AWS
investments_and_gifts.purpose: Statewide higher-ed AI workforce program supporting an AWS expansion; UM participates via collaborative channels (no direct grant).

industry_and_research_partnerships.purpose: NextGenAI consortium membership; research API access and competitive grant pipeline.

industry_and_research_partnerships.partner: University of Arkansas, University of Memphis, University of Tennessee Health Science Center
industry_and_research_partnerships.purpose: Mid-South AI Research Consortium – joint grants, shared digital environments, workforce upskilling.

industry_and_research_partnerships.partner: Texas A&M University, Virginia Tech, Mississippi AI Network

industry_and_research_partnerships.purpose: Statewide AI literacy and workforce training across K-12, community college, and four-year sectors.

industry_and_research_partnerships.partner: AccelerateMS, Mississippi Development Authority, Amazon Web Services
industry_and_research_partnerships.purpose: MAI-TAP statewide AI workforce program tied to AWS expansion.

student_facing_ai.name: Generative AI assistants in first-year writing
student_facing_ai.scope: Department of Writing and Rhetoric first-year writing courses.

student_facing_ai.name: AI research access via NextGenAI (student researchers)
student_facing_ai.scope: Ole Miss researchers (faculty and graduate students) can apply for competitive NextGenAI-funded projects through NCNI/IDS/Practical Ethics.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Mississippi AI Network (MAIN) free statewide AI courses
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: UM is an institutional partner in MAIN, which delivers free, self-paced AI courses statewide and has reached tens of thousands of higher-ed employees and over 1,100 K-12 teachers — a meaningful digital-divide intervention in the Deep South.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: AI ethics education targeting rural and underrepresented institutions
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: The NSF-funded Center for Practical Ethics AI ethics curriculum is designed for rollout through 15 Mississippi community colleges and universities, extending AI literacy beyond elite R1 contexts.

regulatory_context.summary: Mississippi has moved quickly on AI policy at the state level. Governor Tate Reeves issued Executive Order 1584 in January 2025 — Mississippi's first AI executive order — directing the Department of Information Technology Services (ITS) to inventory state agency AI use and develop principles around fairness, privacy, security, transparency, accountability, accessibility, validity, and reliability. S.B. 2426 (2025 regular session) created a state AI Task Force, and ITS has published an Acceptable Use Policy for AI for state agencies. The Mississippi Artificial Intelligence Regulation Task Force (standing up through PEER) has been advised to defer setting binding regulations for now. Mississippi's IHL (Institutions of Higher Learning) system has not published a system-level AI policy; higher-ed AI posture is shaped primarily by state workforce programs (MAI-TAP) and the statewide Mississippi AI Network rather than IHL regulation.

narrative_summary:
The University of Mississippi has built an unusually humanities-forward AI posture for an SEC flagship, with its marquee AI unit, the National Center for Narrative Intelligence (NCNI, founded 2023), focused on how AI can analyze stories and disinformation rather than on generic machine-learning research. That humanities emphasis, combined with the faculty-led AI Task Force chaired by Robert Cummings (Executive Director of Academic Innovation), helped position Ole Miss as one of 15 founding members of OpenAI's NextGenAI consortium in March 2025 — the only Mississippi institution and one of just three SEC universities in the network. A year later, in April 2026, UM co-founded the Mid-South AI Research Consortium with the University of Arkansas, University of Memphis, and UT Health Science Center, aggregating 300+ AI researchers across three states around rural health, supply chain, energy and data centers, agriculture, and defense.

Governance is distributed rather than centralized: there is no chief AI officer, and the AI Task Force has pushed policy-making down to academic departments, with the College of Liberal Arts and School of Journalism and New Media rolling out department-level AI policies through fall 2026. Faculty tooling — the DEER framework for AI-inclusive assignments and a stoplight syllabus template — comes through CETL and the University Libraries, which explicitly discourage AI-detection tools. A new CIO, Victoria Farnsworth, arrived in August 2025 from UT-Chattanooga, where she led the AI program, signaling enterprise-IT readiness to catch up with the academic side.

On the research and workforce sides, UM brings real capacity: the Mississippi Center for Supercomputing Research runs an R1-grade GPU cluster shared across every Mississippi public university; the Center for Practical Ethics holds the first NSF Ethics and Responsible Research grant ever awarded to a Mississippi institution; and UM is embedded in the nation's first statewide AI workforce network (MAIN) and the $9.1M MAI-TAP program tied to an AWS expansion. Gaps remain: no chief AI officer, no published campus-wide AI strategy or AUP, no disclosed enterprise-wide ChatGPT/Copilot deployment, and limited public signal on student-facing AI beyond first-year writing.

gaps_acknowledged: No published campus-wide AI strategy document or formal AI-specific acceptable-use policy was located; inferred posture from task-force activity, library guidance, and departmental policy rollout.

No evidence of a formal enterprise-wide deployment of Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT Edu, or Google Gemini to UM students/faculty. Access to Gemini was referenced as 'under discussion' by the AI Task Force but not confirmed.
No UM-specific AI chatbot or predictive-analytics-for-retention student service identified; only general first-year-writing generative AI pilots.
Institute for Data Science founding year and dedicated funding levels not disclosed on public pages.
UMMC (Jackson) clinical AI detail was out of scope for this Oxford-campus-centered profile; some UMMC AI work exists but is not itemized here.
Specific FTE counts, total dollar commitments, and user counts for most initiatives were not public.

strategic_archetype.rationale: Primary archetype selected from visible public signals: balanced enterprise.
